item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's only works if it's a painting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OP, if the painting is infohazardous, shouldnt it be blocked off in some way? Also that is one smug kitty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This concept reminded me of the cat from Sabrina the Teenage Witch. Also this definitely goes hand in hand with SCP-3312.
+1

item.title: Re: Polished Garbage
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if you're still looking for feedback/help, but here are my two cents: (Sorry if I'm giving bad advice, I'm still fairly new to critizing SCPs.)

+ My thoughts on it.
- Hope this helps!

Honestly, I like this skip as it stands right now, but my main problem with it is that it feels somewhat incomplete.

A decent way of solving this would be by adding more uncanny or interesting changes to the mental transformation process while giving more detail to the physical one, since not only would it make the SCP seem more complete, but it would also make more sense, considering that it's a scientific document and the foundation would probably want to document everything (Or most of it, at least).

I'm not saying that the transformation should be described in painstakingly accurate detail, but going a bit more in depth would help the document seem more official and elaborate. And If you want to shock the readers, you could even add some visceral details such as internal organs changing, although I wouldn't reccomend that, since it would contrast with the lighthearted nature you have going on with the writing.

As for the mental changes, maybe you could add things that relate to the painting. For instance, since it depicts an overweight cat sleeping, perhaps you could make it so people affected by it are prone to become lazier as time goes on, or develop an increased apetite (That would also making sense considering the fact that a transformation like that would require a lot of energy).

Another problem that I had with the article is that it leaves me with quite a few questions, but in a non-satisfactory way. How did the foundation manage to retrieve the painting or create copies without looking at it? Did the people who got turned into cats simply accepted their fate or did they do something to try to revert the changes? Did the foundation run any tests on the cats or the painting? Those are just a few examples of course, but answering them could provide the SCP with a bit more depth.

And since I mentioned tests, I feel like this is an SCP that could benefit from having those due to how it works. You could use those test logs not only as a way of giving the readers more information, but I'm sure you could also add a few jokes in them.

Of course, that was just my opinion, and you're more than free to disagree with me, but I hope that I at least helped you a bit.

item.title: Polished Garbage
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so, this article is now in rewrite. If anyone would like to take a look at it, fix it up, please PM me! My only requirement is that you keep the base idea the same: The painting makes you a cat.

Go forth and polish this garbage.

item.title: Re: Image problems
item.content:encoded: to be more specific, it's a painting by Dennis Blalock. Elisabet Stacy-Hurley just took the photo.

item.title: How To Coldpost
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so, I never gave this SCP a proper celebration, despite it being my first successful SCP. I never got any crits on this thing because I didn't know how to do such a thing, and also didn't want to waste anyone's time.

But now I waste other writers' time on a daily basis, so now I feel free to give this thing a proper inauguration party.

Welcome to the club 3270, you're what started me in this trash pit called the SCP community.

item.title: Re: Image problems
item.content:encoded:
https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0d/BlalockFatCat.jpg

New image was created by Elisabet Stacy-Hurley, who is not me.

Hope this clears things.

item.title: Image problems
item.content:encoded: This image is a poster by graphic designer Sarah Battle. It is not available under Creative Commons. It'll have to come down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like just a watered down version of 3312 without the visceral disturbance that made it work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I share this same feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
made me think of the phrase "curiosity killed the cat"
although no cats were harmed in the making of this SCP

also personally i like this on its own, its a neat concept and its got just the right balance of comedy and serious anomalous effects that i wouldn't call it a -J, but a lot of people seem to think it needs more

this is purely a suggestion but if you want to add more to this SCP i would suggest adding an origin story for the painting, maybe connected with AWCY? bc it feels like they'd do something like this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jokes on that thing, I already jack off to furry porn! *laughter slowly turning into sobbing*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly think this would work better as a -J. The interview is really funny but everything else is kinda basic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmmmm.
Im kinda feelin mixed here.
It's a novel idea and that interview really gave me a chuckle.
But there isn't much.. More.
Ya know?
No-vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it weren’t for the whole “painfully turning into a cat” part, I’d hang the painting in my house somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh…

Nothing here really does it for me. I don't find the anomaly itself at all interesting. It's a thing that does one weird thing, and that's basically it. I couldn't even really crack a smile at the interview. The way I feel about this is pretty much the same way Allison feels about this. I guess it's cute. *shrug*

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview was fun.
That's enough.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3270-1: Curiosity, mostly.

I laughed.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, I've cleared the tags. If you're not going to add all the tags for your article, it's best to just leave the page untagged and wait for an experienced tagger to take care of it. We have a team of people on the site who tag articles, and they search for pages that have no tags. Adding only a few tags prevents the team from finding the article, so if you're unfamiliar with the tags, it's totally fine to wait for them to take care of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By all accounts, this should be downvote fodder, but the ending… the ending got me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted solely for the interview. There are a couple of formatting/tonal/spelling errors here, though. You might want a look at that.
